Alabama.severity_quote: Most foliage on the staked tomato row has collapsed into brown, leafless skeletons while red and green fruit remain hanging on the largely defoliated plants.
Alabama.lesion_morphology: Individual lesions are not resolvable at this field-scale distance; the canopy reads as wholesale browning and shriveling of leaves rather than discrete spots.
Alabama.lesion_morphology_quote: Foliage appears uniformly necrotic and dried, with bare stems exposed instead of visible bulls-eye spots.
Alabama.affected_organs_quote: Leaves across the row are brown and desiccated and the supporting stems/branches are visibly bare and darkened.
Alabama.spread_pattern_quote: The entire staked tomato row shows defoliation top-to-bottom while adjacent weeds/ground cover stay green, indicating uniform progression through the planting.
variations_from_canonical: Image shows whole-plant defoliation and canopy collapse rather than the small 1/4–1/2 inch bulls-eye lesions on lower leaves described canonically.
No yellow halo or concentric-ring detail is visible at this scale — disease is past the diagnostic-lesion stage.
Fruit in the image still appears intact and ripening on the vine; the canonical sunken, leathery calyx-end fruit lesions are not evident here.
Damage is not confined to the lower canopy as canonical text suggests; the upper canopy is equally defoliated.

Connecticut.severity_quote: A few well-developed dark lesions are present on a still mostly green leaf, with adjacent leaves showing yellowing and one larger necrotic patch near the leaf tip.
Connecticut.lesion_morphology: Roughly circular dark brown to black spots about 5–10 mm across with faint concentric rings and a tan/gray center, some surrounded by mild chlorotic yellowing; one larger coalesced necrotic patch sits at a leaflet tip.
Connecticut.lesion_morphology_quote: Two small round dark lesions on the central leaflet show concentric ring patterning, and a larger irregular brown necrotic area occupies the tip of an adjacent leaflet.
Connecticut.affected_organs_quote: Lesions and chlorosis are visible on the tomato leaflets in the foreground; green fruit in the background appears unblemished.
Connecticut.spread_pattern_quote: A few discrete spots are scattered across one leaflet while neighboring lower leaves show yellowing and isolated necrotic flecks rather than uniform coverage.
variations_from_canonical: The clear bulls-eye concentric ring pattern is only weakly visible in the small lesions; rings are faint rather than pronounced.
The yellow halo around individual spots is subtle/absent on the central leaflet — chlorosis appears more as broad leaf yellowing than as a tight halo around each lesion.
Visible lesions are larger than the canonical 1/4 inch (~6 mm) — the tip lesion is a coalesced patch >2 cm across.
No stem or fruit lesions are visible; the green fruits in the background appear clean, so this image only documents foliar symptoms.

Alabama.severity_quote: Entire rows of staked tomato plants appear stunted, drooping, and sparsely foliated compared to expected canopy fill for trellised tomatoes at this growth stage.
Alabama.lesion_morphology: No discrete lesions are resolvable at this field-scale distance; foliage appears overall dark green with a wilted, droopy posture and reduced leaf size rather than showing distinct spots or mosaic patches.
Alabama.lesion_morphology_quote: Leaves on the affected plants look limp and downward-hanging with no clearly visible discrete lesions or color-broken patches at this resolution.
Alabama.affected_organs_quote: Whole plants on the white plastic mulch beds show drooping, stunted foliage along the entire row, with no fruit or flowers clearly visible.
Alabama.spread_pattern_quote: Multiple parallel staked rows show consistent stunting and droop along their full length, indicating a field-wide rather than localized distribution.
variations_from_canonical: Image is a wide field shot showing whole-plant stunting and droopy posture rather than the close-up mosaic, yellow streaking, or ring-spot foliar patterns emphasized in the canonical description.
Filiformity (shoestring leaves), the canonical hallmark for CMV in tomato, is not resolvable in this distant field view.
No fruit, flower color breaking, or vein yellowing is visible in this image, even though the canonical text lists those as common CMV signs.

Virginia.severity_quote: Two entire tomato shoots show severe, plant-wide leaf distortion with the foliage reduced to thin, shriveled, shoestring-like remnants.
Virginia.lesion_morphology: No discrete lesions are visible; instead the leaves are extremely narrow, filiform, twisted and crinkled, with greatly reduced leaf blades that appear uniformly green rather than mottled.
Virginia.lesion_morphology_quote: Leaflets are reduced to thread- and shoestring-like strips with curled, puckered margins along otherwise intact green stems.
Virginia.affected_organs_quote: Both detached shoots show distorted, filiform leaflets along the entire length of the stems, while the stems themselves remain green and structurally intact.
Virginia.spread_pattern_quote: Distortion is consistent from the basal leaves to the growing tips on both shoots, not localized to any one zone of the canopy.
variations_from_canonical: The dominant visible symptom is filiformity/shoestring leaves; the canonical mosaic of light-and-dark green patches is not apparent in this image.
No ring-spots, line patterns, yellow streaking, or flower color breaking are visible on the tissue shown.
Leaves appear largely uniform green rather than displaying the yellow-and-green mosaic emphasized in the canonical summary.

Symptoms shown are at the severe end of the canonical description — the diagnostic 'thin, shoestring-like leaves' feature is expressed here in an advanced, plant-wide form rather than as a subtle finding.

Alabama.severity_quote: An uprooted whole tomato plant is laid out on a tray with extensively wilted, browned, and necrotic foliage and pale, elongated lesions running along the main stem.
Alabama.lesion_morphology: Pale tan to cream-colored, elongated streak-like cankers running longitudinally along the green stems, with adjacent tissue appearing bleached and shredded; no fruit spots are visible.
Alabama.lesion_morphology_quote: The main stem shows long, light-colored split/streak lesions with frayed pale tissue, while side stems show similar bleached longitudinal stripes.
Alabama.affected_organs_quote: Stems display longitudinal pale cankers, leaves are wilted with brown necrotic patches and curling, and the exposed root system is visible at the base of the uprooted plant.
Alabama.spread_pattern_quote: Symptoms involve the entire plant — stems, lower and upper leaves all show wilting and necrosis, consistent with systemic vascular collapse rather than a localized lesion zone.
variations_from_canonical: No 'bird's eye spots' on fruit are visible — no fruit are present in the frame, so the canonical fruit-spot diagnostic cannot be observed.

Stem cankers here appear as long pale longitudinal splits/streaks rather than the canonical description of stems splitting to reveal yellow-to-dark-reddish-brown internal vascular discoloration; no obvious dark vascular browning is exposed.

The image shows the plant uprooted and laid out on a tray for diagnostic display rather than in-situ field symptoms, so canopy-level patterns like one-sided wilt or marginal 'firing' cannot be assessed from natural orientation.
Severity is more advanced (whole-plant collapse and necrosis) than the canonical 'initial symptoms' description of curling/chlorosis of lower leaves.

Kentucky.severity_quote: Multiple uprooted tomato plants are piled in a cardboard box, with extensive wilting, browning, and desiccation of foliage on still-green stems.
Kentucky.lesion_morphology: No discrete lesions or fruit spots are visible; the visible damage is generalized leaflet browning, curling, and shriveling along otherwise intact green stems and petioles.
Kentucky.lesion_morphology_quote: Leaves appear crisped and brown along the margins inward while the main stems remain green and turgid, consistent with vascular wilt rather than localized leaf spotting.
Kentucky.affected_organs_quote: Whole tomato plants have been pulled and bundled, showing wilted, scorched leaves on green stems with no fruit visible in the frame.
Kentucky.spread_pattern_quote: The damage affects entire plants uniformly rather than being confined to lower leaves or one side, with multiple plants showing the same wilt.
variations_from_canonical: No 'bird's eye' fruit spots are observable because no fruit is in frame.
No stem splitting, cankers, or visible vascular discoloration can be seen on the exterior of the green stems.
Image documents post-rogueing/disposal of symptomatic plants in a box rather than in-field early symptoms like leaf curling or one-sided wilt.
Leaf damage presents as generalized marginal scorch and desiccation rather than discrete white blister-like spots with dark rings.

Alabama.severity_quote: Three entire tomato plants have been pulled from the soil and are completely collapsed, with desiccated brown foliage and dead stems indicating total plant death.
Alabama.lesion_morphology: Discrete lesions are not resolvable; instead the lower stems appear bleached/tan and shredded with dried brown tissue, and the foliage is uniformly browned and crispy rather than showing isolated spots.
Alabama.lesion_morphology_quote: The basal stems show pale tan, dried, frayed bark and the leaves are uniformly necrotic across each plant.
Alabama.affected_organs_quote: Each uprooted specimen shows browned/dead leaves, collapsed stems, and exposed root systems still attached, with only a few green fruit remaining on the middle plant.
Alabama.spread_pattern_quote: All three plants are uniformly dead from root to shoot rather than showing a gradient from lower to upper canopy.
variations_from_canonical: No white fungal mycelial mat is visible on the stems or roots in this photograph.
No tan to reddish-brown spherical sclerotia (1–2 mm) are discernible on stem bases or surrounding soil at this image resolution.

Image is a posed display of three uprooted plants on a car hood rather than an in-situ field shot, so the canonical 'water-soaked lesion' early stage is not represented — only the terminal whole-plant death endpoint.
Fruit lesions described in the canonical text (water-soaked, soft, star-shaped) are not visible; the few remaining fruits appear green and intact.

Florida.severity_quote: Dense white mycelial mat covers the substrate with abundant sclerotia formation, indicating advanced fungal colonization.
Florida.lesion_morphology: Numerous small, spherical sclerotia (~1 mm) ranging from pale tan/cream to mustard-yellow and darker reddish-brown, scattered across a brown substrate webbed with fluffy white mycelium.
Florida.lesion_morphology_quote: Tiny round bead-like sclerotia in mixed tan, yellow-ochre and brown shades sit on top of a feathery white mycelial mat.
Florida.affected_organs_quote: The image shows a culture/plate setup rather than living plant tissue — fungal growth is on a brown rectangular substrate inside a glass petri-style dish.
Florida.spread_pattern_quote: Mycelium and sclerotia cover the substrate broadly with sclerotia distributed densely across most of the surface.
variations_from_canonical: This is a laboratory/culture image of the pathogen on a substrate in a petri dish, not a field photograph of an infected tomato plant.
No tomato foliage, stem, root, or fruit tissue is visible — the canonical organs (foliar, stem, root, whole plant) cannot be assessed from this image.
Sclerotia here appear in a wider color range including pale yellow/mustard tones, not only the canonical 'tan to reddish-brown'.
Sclerotia look smaller than the canonical 1–2 mm size, appearing closer to ~1 mm or less.
Canonical signs such as water-soaked lesions, wilting, yellowing, or star-shaped fruit lesions are not visible because no host tissue is shown.

Alabama.severity_quote: A single large brown lesion covers roughly the upper third of an otherwise still-green, firm-looking fruit, with no visible soft rot or collapse.
Alabama.lesion_morphology: A large, roughly circular brown lesion with clearly defined concentric rings of lighter and darker brown discoloration radiating from a central point, with smooth, gradient margins that fade into the green surface.
Alabama.lesion_morphology_quote: The lesion shows nested target-like rings of light and dark brown on the shoulder of the fruit, with a glossy, intact skin and no rupturing.
Alabama.affected_organs_quote: Only a single green tomato fruit is visible against a blue background; no foliage, stem, or pod is shown.
Alabama.spread_pattern: single coalesced lesion on one fruit
Alabama.spread_pattern_quote: One large lesion occupies the top/shoulder of the fruit; the rest of the fruit surface is unaffected and uniformly green.
variations_from_canonical: Canonical lists 'Pod' as the affected part, but this image shows the lesion on a tomato fruit, not a pod.
Lesion appears firm and smooth-skinned with no soft, decayed area visible, consistent with the canonical 'firm, smooth margins' early stage rather than late soft rot.
Lesion is positioned on the upper shoulder of the fruit rather than the soil-contact underside described as typical in the canonical reference.

North Carolina.severity_quote: Several plants in the foreground rows show extensive wilting and collapse, with sparse, drooping foliage compared to healthier-looking neighbors.
North Carolina.affected_organs_quote: Affected plants exhibit overall wilt and collapse of the canopy with drooping leaves and stems, while no individual fruit lesions are resolvable in this wide field shot.
North Carolina.spread_pattern_quote: Wilted/dying plants are interspersed among healthier-looking plants along the staked rows on red and black plastic mulch, with no clear edge-of-row pattern.
variations_from_canonical: Image is a wide-angle field view of staked tomato plants on plasticulture beds; no fruit lesions are visible, contrary to the canonical fruit-lesion description.
Symptoms shown are whole-plant wilting/collapse rather than the round/oblong concentric-ring fruit lesions described in canonical reference.
Distribution appears as scattered plant decline across rows rather than localized fruit-soil contact damage.

Alabama.severity_quote: All three green tomato fruits show large coalescing dark blotches covering 30-60% of each fruit's surface, with one fruit showing a cracked/sunken necrotic area exposing tan tissue.
Alabama.lesion_morphology: Large, irregular, greasy-looking dark brown to grayish-olive blotches with diffuse, indistinct margins covering broad areas of the fruit shoulder and side, firm in appearance with one lesion showing a sunken cracked tan necrotic patch.
Alabama.lesion_morphology_quote: The lesions are broad, smooth, dark olive-brown patches that blend gradually into the surrounding green skin rather than forming sharp circular spots.
Alabama.affected_organs_quote: Three immature green tomato fruits are shown against a black background, each with extensive dark blotchy discoloration; no leaves or stems are visible in the frame.
Alabama.spread_pattern_quote: Lesions are distributed across the upper shoulder and lateral surfaces of each fruit, with one lesion wrapping toward the calyx and another extending into a sunken crack.
variations_from_canonical: Lesions appear as large diffuse blotches rather than the canonical 'firm, dark brown, circular spots' — margins are indistinct and shapes are irregular rather than circular.
No thin powdery white fungal/mycelial sporulation is visible on the fruit surfaces despite the advanced lesion development.
Only fruit are shown; the canonical foliar, stem, and root symptoms cannot be evaluated from this image.
One fruit shows a cracked, sunken tan/cream-colored necrotic patch — a more advanced breakdown than the 'firm' surface described in the canonical text.

Connecticut.severity_quote: Multiple leaflets show large necrotic tan-brown patches that consume more than half of each affected leaflet, with surrounding canopy already collapsed and dried.
Connecticut.lesion_morphology: Large irregular tan to grayish-brown necrotic blotches with diffuse darker margins crossing major veins, occupying the leaflet tips and edges, with papery dried tissue and tearing at the lesion centers.
Connecticut.lesion_morphology_quote: Tan-brown blotches with feathery dark edges spread across leaflets and are not stopped by the midrib, with brittle dried-out centers.
Connecticut.affected_organs_quote: Several tomato leaflets carry expansive brown necrotic patches and the surrounding stems/petioles bear dried, blighted tissue; a green unripe fruit visible behind the foliage shows no obvious lesion.
Connecticut.spread_pattern_quote: Blighted leaflets are interspersed with still-green leaves on the same branches, and dead brown foliage in the background suggests the infection is progressing through the plant rather than confined to one stratum.
variations_from_canonical: No thin powdery white sporulation/mycelium is visible on the lesion surfaces in this image — the affected tissue appears dry and papery rather than fuzzy.
Lesions are tan to grayish-brown rather than the canonical 'dark brown'; the green-gray water-soaked advancing edge is only faintly suggested.
No fruit or tuber symptoms are visible — the one tomato fruit in frame is green and apparently unblemished, despite the canonical description noting firm dark brown shoulder spots on fruit.
Damage looks more dried/necrotic than actively expanding, suggesting drier conditions at the time of photo than the humid sporulating phase the canonical text emphasizes.

Alabama.severity_quote: Each of the three detached leaves shows numerous coalescing brown patches across most leaflets, but the leaf tissue itself remains green and intact rather than withered.
Alabama.lesion_morphology: Diffuse, irregular tan-to-brown velvety patches with fuzzy, indistinct margins, ranging from small dots to larger coalescing blotches on the lower leaf surface.
Alabama.lesion_morphology_quote: The patches look fuzzy and powdery rather than sharply defined, with several small spots merging into larger irregular zones.
Alabama.affected_organs_quote: Only detached compound tomato leaves are visible, each showing brown velvety patches scattered across the leaflet undersides.
Alabama.spread_pattern_quote: Lesions are distributed broadly over nearly every leaflet of all three leaves rather than being concentrated at margins or tips.
variations_from_canonical: Image shows the lower (abaxial) leaf surface only — the canonical pale greenish-yellow upper-surface spots cannot be confirmed here.
Mold color in this photo reads as tan-to-medium-brown rather than the canonical olive-green velvety appearance.
Despite extensive lesion coverage, the leaves are not visibly withered or dried as the canonical description suggests for advanced infection.
No stem or fruit symptoms are visible — only foliar involvement is documented in this image, even though the canonical reference also lists stem and pod.

Connecticut.severity_quote: Several discrete patches of velvety mold are visible on the underside of a single detached leaf, but the leaf tissue is still largely green and intact.
Connecticut.lesion_morphology: Diffuse, irregular grayish-tan to olive-brown velvety patches with soft, feathered margins, ranging roughly from small spots to broader 1–2 cm areas, lacking sharp borders.
Connecticut.lesion_morphology_quote: The lesions appear as fuzzy tan-brown smudges on the lower leaf surface, blending gradually into surrounding green tissue rather than forming defined rings.
Connecticut.affected_organs_quote: The image shows the underside of a compound tomato leaf with multiple velvety mold patches scattered across leaflets; no stems or fruit are visible.
Connecticut.spread_pattern_quote: Multiple separate fuzzy patches are distributed across several leaflets of the same leaf, rather than being confined to one area.
variations_from_canonical: Mold color in this image reads as grayish-tan to light brown rather than the canonical 'olive-green to brown' velvety mold.
No corresponding upper-surface yellow spots are visible because only the abaxial (under) side of the leaf is shown.
Lesion patches are diffuse and feathered rather than appearing as 'discrete' spots described in the canonical reference.
Only foliar tissue is shown; stem and fruit symptoms listed in the canonical description are not visible.

Alabama.severity_quote: Numerous small dark specks are distributed across multiple leaflets with one upper leaflet showing extensive coalescing chlorosis, but the foliage is still largely intact and green.
Alabama.lesion_morphology: Very small (pinpoint to ~1–2 mm) dark brown to black speck-like spots scattered over the leaf lamina, with surrounding tissue turning yellow on the most heavily affected leaflet, producing diffuse chlorotic halos rather than crisp angular margins.
Alabama.lesion_morphology_quote: Tiny black flecks pepper the leaflets, and the central upper leaflet shows yellow interveinal blotches studded with these dark specks.
Alabama.affected_organs_quote: Multiple compound-leaf leaflets carry dark specks, and a darkened necrotic patch is visible at a petiole junction near the central stem.
Alabama.spread_pattern: scattered across multiple leaflets with one focal hotspot
Alabama.spread_pattern_quote: Specks are sprinkled across most leaflets in the frame, but density and chlorosis are concentrated on a single upper-central leaflet.
variations_from_canonical: Halos on the worst leaflet appear as broad diffuse yellow blotches rather than the small discrete yellow halos described canonically.
Lesion margins are irregular/rounded rather than the 'angular, straight-edged' shape called out in the canonical diagnostic features.
A dark necrotic lesion is visible on the petiole/stem area, which is consistent with canonical 'stem' involvement but is more conspicuous than the foliar-dominant emphasis of the canonical summary.
No fruit is present in this image, so the canonical fruit-speck diagnostic cannot be evaluated here.

North Carolina.severity_quote: A single curled tomato leaflet is densely peppered with dozens of dark lesions and shows tissue tearing/holes, indicating heavy infection rather than early speckling.
North Carolina.lesion_morphology: Numerous small, roughly circular dark brown to black spots, many with thin pale/tan centers and faint chlorotic-purple halos, coalescing in places into larger irregular necrotic patches.
North Carolina.lesion_morphology_quote: Spots are small, dark and round, some with lighter dried centers, scattered densely across the lamina with occasional merging into bigger lesions.
North Carolina.affected_organs_quote: Only a single curled, distorted tomato leaflet is visible, covered in dark specking; no stems, fruit or roots are shown.
North Carolina.spread_pattern_quote: Lesions are distributed broadly over the entire leaf surface from base to tip rather than concentrated at margins or midrib.
variations_from_canonical: Lesions appear more rounded than the canonical 'angular, straight-edged' description.
Substantial leaf curling and tissue tearing/holes are visible — heavier blade distortion than the canonical description implies.
Halos look more dull purple/brown than the canonical 'yellow halo'.
Lesion density is much higher than typical canonical photos — coalescing into larger necrotic patches rather than remaining discrete pinpoint specks.

Alabama.severity_quote: A single tomato leaflet shows extensive but not coalescing fine flecking with intact lamina and no visible wilting or collapse.
Alabama.lesion_morphology: Numerous pinpoint to small (~1 mm) dark brown to purplish-brown specks and flecks with diffuse, blurred margins scattered over a dull, bronzed grayish-green leaf surface.
Alabama.lesion_morphology_quote: The upper leaf surface is peppered with tiny dark purple-brown stipples on a bronzed background, with no sharply bordered spots.
Alabama.affected_organs_quote: Only foliage is shown — a compound tomato leaflet with bronzing and dark stippling across the lamina.
Alabama.spread_pattern_quote: The fine flecks are distributed fairly evenly between veins across the entire visible leaflet rather than concentrated at margins or tips.
variations_from_canonical: Lesions in this image are pinpoint stipples/flecks rather than the discrete 'small brown lesions' described canonically.
No 'shepherd's crook', leaf rolling, or wilting is visible — the leaf is flat and intact despite heavy stippling.
No fruit, stem, or whole-plant symptoms are visible; only a single leaflet is shown, so the canonical multi-organ involvement is not represented.
Bronzing is present but appears as a dull grayish cast across the whole leaflet rather than being limited to 'upper sides of young leaves'.

Delaware.severity_quote: Two detached compound leaves rest on a white background showing patchy bronzing and necrotic blotches across roughly 20–40% of the leaflet area, with margins curling but most leaflet tissue still green.
Delaware.lesion_morphology: Irregular bronze-to-brown necrotic blotches with diffuse, blurred margins concentrated along the midrib and leaflet tips, accompanied by scattered tiny dark stipple-like flecks on otherwise green tissue.
Delaware.lesion_morphology_quote: Brown necrotic patches blend gradually into surrounding green tissue without sharply defined edges, and small pinpoint dark spots are visible across the leaflets.
Delaware.affected_organs_quote: Bronzing, necrosis, and downward cupping are visible only on the leaflets and along the leaf rachis; no stems, fruit, or whole-plant view are present.
Delaware.spread_pattern: scattered within-leaf, concentrated toward leaflet tips and one side of the rachis
Delaware.spread_pattern_quote: Necrotic blotches are unevenly distributed — heavier on the smaller left leaf and on the upper/distal leaflets of the right leaf, with basal leaflets remaining largely green.
variations_from_canonical: Image shows detached, isolated compound leaves on a studio background rather than an in-situ whole plant or seedling, so no 'shepherd's crook' or wilt posture is visible.
Lesions appear as larger irregular bronze-brown blotches rather than the canonical 'small brown lesions' or chlorotic ring spots; no concentric rings are visible.
No fruit, stem, or ringspot symptoms shown — only foliar bronzing, necrosis, and downward leaflet cupping are documented here.
Bronzing is patchy and mid-leaf rather than uniformly covering the upper surface of the youngest leaves as canonically described.

Alabama.severity_quote: A longitudinally split stem and side branch reveal extensive brown discoloration filling the entire pith and vascular core along the full length of the specimen.
Alabama.lesion_morphology: Continuous tan-to-medium-brown internal discoloration occupying the pith and vascular cylinder, bordered by thin intact green outer cortex strips, with a dry, slightly sunken texture rather than discrete spots.
Alabama.lesion_morphology_quote: The split stem shows a uniform brown core running the length of both the main stem and the lateral branch, framed by narrow green epidermal margins.
Alabama.affected_organs_quote: Brown discoloration is visible inside the longitudinally cut stem and its branching shoot, involving both the vascular ring and the central pith.
Alabama.spread_pattern_quote: The browning extends continuously and uninterrupted from the cut base through the node and into the lateral branch.
variations_from_canonical: No bacterial ooze, beads, or wet slime are visible at the cut surfaces in this image — the interior appears dry and matte rather than the slimy tan-white ooze described canonically.
Discoloration is a fairly uniform medium brown rather than the canonical progression from light tan/yellow-brown to dark brown-black streaking; no distinct streak pattern is visible.
No foliage, roots, basal cankers, or whole-plant wilt are shown — the image is restricted to a dissected stem segment, so the canonical foliar-wilt and root-rot signs cannot be assessed here.

Browning involves the entire pith and vascular core together rather than appearing as a distinct vascular ring, suggesting a more advanced internal collapse than the early canonical 'light tan vascular discoloration' stage.

Georgia.severity_quote: A cut tomato stem section is suspended over a clear glass beaker of water for the bacterial ooze streaming test, so disease severity in a whole-plant sense is not depicted.
Georgia.lesion_morphology: The cut stem end is mostly green externally but reveals a tan to light-brown discolored vascular/cortex region with a small whitish-tan ooze bead emerging from the cut surface on the lower right.
Georgia.lesion_morphology_quote: Tan-brown discoloration is visible at the cut face and a pale, sticky-looking droplet protrudes from the exposed vascular tissue.
Georgia.affected_organs_quote: Only a severed stem segment is shown; the cut end exposes brown-tinged vascular/pith tissue with bacterial ooze.
Georgia.spread_pattern_quote: Discoloration and ooze are confined to the cut end of an isolated stem piece; no canopy or row context is visible.
variations_from_canonical: Image is a laboratory/diagnostic ooze-test demonstration rather than a field photo of wilting plants.
No foliage, roots, or whole-plant wilting are visible — only an excised stem segment.
The classic milky ooze 'thread' descending into water described in the canonical text is not yet clearly formed; only a small bead is visible at the cut surface.
External stem surface is still green with no obvious basal canker, contrasting with the canonical 'brown cankers at the base.'

Delaware.severity_quote: Numerous small dark spots cover most of the leaflets, with extensive necrotic patches, marginal browning, and tissue tearing along several leaflet edges.
Delaware.lesion_morphology: Small (~1–3 mm) circular dark-brown to black spots densely peppering the leaflets, many coalescing into larger irregular tan-brown necrotic blotches along leaf margins; the small spots show dark centers but only minimal yellow halos.
Delaware.lesion_morphology_quote: Tiny dark pinpoint lesions are scattered across the green leaflet surfaces, while several leaflets show larger merged tan/brown dead patches and chewed-looking necrotic edges.
Delaware.affected_organs_quote: Spots and necrotic patches are confined to the leaflet blades; the visible green petiole and stem appear clean and unblemished.
Delaware.spread_pattern_quote: Every leaflet on the compound leaf shows lesions, with spots distributed broadly across each blade rather than clustered in one zone.
variations_from_canonical: Lesions appear darker brown/black and smaller (pinpoint, ~1–2 mm) than the canonical tan-to-gray 2–5 mm spots.
Yellow halos around individual spots are not prominent in this image; surrounding tissue is mostly still green.
Several leaflets show large coalesced necrotic patches and torn/missing tissue along margins, beyond the discrete circular spots described canonically.
The visible petiole/stem shows no lesions, though canonical text notes stems can be affected.

Connecticut.severity_quote: The single leaflet is densely peppered with dozens of lesions across nearly its entire surface, with marginal yellowing, edge necrosis, and at least one shot-hole where lesion tissue has dropped out.
Connecticut.lesion_morphology: Numerous small (~2-4 mm) circular spots with pale tan-to-gray centers and slightly darker brown margins, scattered across the upper leaf surface; halos are faint or absent and individual lesions remain discrete (non-coalescing).
Connecticut.lesion_morphology_quote: Small round gray-centered spots with darker rims dot the lamina between veins; a few lesions near the margin have darkened and necrotic tissue has fallen out.
Connecticut.affected_organs_quote: Only a detached tomato leaflet is shown; lesions cover the leaf blade and extend to the margins, while no stem or fruit tissue is visible in the frame.
Connecticut.spread_pattern_quote: Spots are distributed fairly evenly across the entire leaflet surface from base to tip rather than clustered on one zone, with marginal yellowing concentrated at the leaf edges.
variations_from_canonical: Image is a detached single leaflet on a red studio cloth, so the canonical 'lower-canopy-first, progressing upward' spread pattern cannot be observed.
Pronounced bright yellow halos around each lesion, as described canonically, are not obvious here — halos are faint and diffuse, with yellowing instead concentrated along the leaf margin.
Dark raised pycnidia in lesion centers are not clearly resolvable at this image scale, though canonical description treats them as a key diagnostic feature.
At least one lesion has progressed to a shot-hole (tissue dropout), which the canonical text does not specifically describe for Septoria.
No stem or fruit lesions are visible in this image, so the canonical mention of stem/seed involvement cannot be confirmed from this photo.

Kentucky.severity_quote: A single green tomato fruit shows a large central decayed crater with ruptured skin and a visible white fungal mycelium plug, indicating well-established fruit rot.
Kentucky.lesion_morphology: A roughly circular sunken lesion at the stem-end of an unripe green fruit, ~1.5 inches across, with tan-to-pale-brown necrotic flaps of split skin radiating from the center and a dense whitish fungal growth filling the cavity.
Kentucky.lesion_morphology_quote: The skin has ruptured into star-shaped tan flaps surrounding a soft white moldy center on an otherwise green fruit.
Kentucky.affected_organs_quote: Only a detached green tomato fruit is shown, exhibiting a central rotted area with white mycelium; no leaves or stems are visible.
Kentucky.spread_pattern_quote: The image shows one isolated fruit on pavement next to a ruler, so canopy or row spread cannot be assessed.
variations_from_canonical: Visible fungal growth in the cavity is whitish-cottony rather than the canonical 'dense velvety gray brown spore mass'.
Fruit is unripe (green) rather than the typical reddening/ripe fruit context, and skin has ruptured outward in star-shaped tan flaps rather than a uniform whitish soft rot.
No 'ghost spots' (small 3-8 mm whitish rings) are visible on the surrounding fruit surface.
Image shows only an isolated fruit with a ruler for scale; foliar, stem, and concentric-ring girdling features described in the canonical text are not represented.

Mississippi.severity_quote: A single leaflet shows a large tan necrotic wedge covering roughly the lower third to half of the leaflet, with a second fully blighted brown leaflet visible on an adjacent stem.
Mississippi.lesion_morphology: A large V-shaped tan-to-light-brown necrotic lesion entering from the leaflet tip and margin, with a darker brown desiccated tip, a sharply defined boundary against still-green tissue, and faint concentric tonal banding within the dead area.
Mississippi.lesion_morphology_quote: The lesion is a broad wedge of papery tan tissue with a darker brown collapsed apex, bordered abruptly by healthy green leaf blade.
Mississippi.affected_organs_quote: Two tomato leaflets show the damage — one with a wedge of dead tissue at its tip, the other entirely collapsed to brown — while stems and any fruit in view appear green and unaffected.
Mississippi.spread_pattern_quote: Only isolated leaflets on the plant are affected; surrounding foliage in the frame remains green and healthy.
variations_from_canonical: No velvety gray-brown sporulating mass is visible on the lesion — the dead tissue appears dry and papery rather than fuzzy with spores.
No stem lesion or concentric girdling rings are visible; damage in this image is confined to leaflets.
No fruit symptoms (whitish soft rot or ghost-spot rings) are visible — the green fruit in the frame appears unblemished.
Lesion entry is from the leaflet tip/margin as a broad wedge rather than the small tan-to-brown spots emphasized in the canonical description.

Louisiana.severity_quote: A central plant has fully collapsed with a dried, brown stem and desiccated hanging foliage, while neighboring plants remain green.
Louisiana.lesion_morphology: No discrete lesions are visible; the affected stem is shriveled and tan-brown along its length with collapsed, papery dried leaves attached, rather than showing clear margined spots.
Louisiana.lesion_morphology_quote: The diseased stem appears shrunken and uniformly brown, with brittle brown leaflets hanging from it.
Louisiana.affected_organs_quote: The main stem and all of its attached foliage are dead and brown, indicating loss of the entire shoot.
Louisiana.spread_pattern_quote: A single plant within the staked row on black plastic mulch is killed while plants on either side remain healthy and green.
variations_from_canonical: No white cottony mycelium is visible on the affected tissue in this image.
No large irregular black sclerotia are visible on the stem or surrounding tissue.
Affected stem is tan-brown and shriveled rather than the bleached white appearance described in the canonical reference.
No flowers or fruits are visible, so flower-stage water-soaking and gray fruit rot cannot be confirmed here.
Image documents the end-stage collapse/wilt of an individual plant rather than the diagnostic mold-and-sclerotia signs.

Maine.severity_quote: A main stem section is bleached tan and shredded/dried at the lesion zone while a tuft of white cottony mycelium colonizes the stem junction.
Maine.lesion_morphology: A girdling stem lesion at a branch junction with bleached light-tan, frayed/shredded outer tissue on one side and a discrete pad of dense white cottony mycelium on the green side, with no clearly visible black sclerotia.
Maine.lesion_morphology_quote: The right portion of the main stem is dry, bleached tan and split open in fibrous strands, while a clump of white fluffy fungal growth sits at the node.
Maine.affected_organs_quote: The lower main stem is bleached and disintegrating, white mycelium is centered at the stem-branch joint, and the attached leaves are wilted and collapsed.
Maine.spread_pattern_quote: Damage is concentrated at one branching node on the main stem, with disease progressing outward along the stem from that single point.
variations_from_canonical: Image is a studio/laboratory photograph of an excised stem on a gray background, not an in-field flowering plant.
No black sclerotia are visible — only white cottony mycelium and bleached stem tissue are evident.
No flowers or fruit are visible in this image, so the canonical 'water-soaked flowers' and 'gray rotting fruit' signs cannot be confirmed here.
The bleached stem tissue is markedly frayed and shredded into fibrous strands, more disintegrated than the canonical 'dry, bleached' description implies.

Kentucky.severity_quote: The entire plant has been uprooted and laid on a cloth; nearly all foliage is brown, curled, and desiccated, indicating whole-plant collapse rather than a localized lesion.
Kentucky.lesion_morphology: No discrete fungal lesion is clearly resolved; the plant shows widespread leaf browning, shriveling and curling along stems, plus one darkened, shriveled fruit at upper right with a brown, sunken surface.
Kentucky.lesion_morphology_quote: Leaves are uniformly tan-brown and crisped, and a single fruit is collapsed and dark brown without any visible white cottony tuft or sporulating crown.
Kentucky.affected_organs_quote: Foliage is fully wilted and desiccated, the stem and side branches show dry necrotic tissue, and one fruit on an upper truss is collapsed and brown.
Kentucky.spread_pattern_quote: Symptoms cover the entire excavated plant uniformly from base to tip rather than being confined to a few fruit or a lower-canopy zone.
variations_from_canonical: Canonical describes water-soaked fruit lesions covered with a thin cotton-like white mycelial tuft and dark sporulation crowning it — none of those features is visible in this image.
No coarse fungal hyphal strands or 'nests of mold' linking adjacent fruit are visible; only one shriveled fruit is shown.
Image presents as a whole-plant collapse with desiccated foliage and stem, whereas the canonical reference focuses on fruit/root rot morphology rather than systemic foliar wilt.
The plant has been uprooted and posed on a cloth with a ruler, so the field spread pattern cannot be observed and is inferred only from the specimen.
